topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a sharp, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon. My thumb hovered over the screen, a familiar tightening in my chest. It was Professor Albright. A photo of a campus event, a casual smile that felt suddenly predatory. I stared at it, my jaw clenching so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I’d spent the last hour meticulously crafting a response—something witty, something that would put a little distance between us, something cool. But the words wouldn't form. Instead, I just closed the app, the screen’s blue light stinging my eyes. I stood up, pacing the small confines of my dorm room, the carpet fibers seeming to mock my restlessness. I needed to ignore it. I needed to pretend this digital tether didn't exist.

[story 2]

I found myself checking my phone every thirty seconds, a compulsive habit I hadn't possessed before. Professor Albright’s profile picture stared back at me from the screen, framed by a backdrop I vaguely recognized from a lecture slide. A strange heat bloomed in my cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with the room temperature. I tried to focus on the calculus textbook spread across my desk, but my gaze kept drifting back to the glowing rectangle. My fingers began to tap an uneven rhythm on the wooden surface of my desk, a nervous energy vibrating through my hand. Why was this happening? I wanted to simply put the device down, to shut the whole thing off, but a stubborn insistence kept me rooted, forcing me to keep looking, to analyze the subtle shifts in his online presence.

[story 3]

The email arrived, innocuous enough—a reminder about the upcoming seminar. But beneath the professional veneer, there was a distinct undercurrent of scrutiny. I reread the message three times, the words swimming before my eyes. I felt a profound sense of being watched, a prickling sensation along the nape of my neck. I picked up my pen, intending to write a simple confirmation, but my hand shook so badly the ink bled slightly onto the paper. I pushed the notebook away, needing space, needing to prove that this digital intrusion hadn't derailed my composure. I walked to the window, watching the distant campus lights, trying to settle a tremor that seemed to run from my core right up to my fingertips.
